commonName: Pot Marigold (not to be confused with Tagetes marigolds

range.nativeRange: Mediterranean region (southern Europe, northern Africa); cultivated globally for thousands of years; one of the oldest cultivated ornamentals and medicinal herbs

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to North America; introduced and occasionally naturalized in disturbed areas across the US

invasive.description: Not considered invasive in the lower 48 states; self-seeds freely but does not spread aggressively beyond cultivated areas; not listed by any US state as a noxious weed or invasive species

zone.description: Annual in most zones; self-sows to maintain a colony in Zones 5-8; may overwinter as a biennial in Zone 9-10; short-lived perennial in Zone 11

description.spread: 12-18" per plant; self-seeds to form a colony
description.growthRate: Fast annual; blooms in 50-70 days from seed
description.timeToMaturity: First blooms 50-70 days from direct sow; essentially continuous from then until hard frost
description.lifespan: Annual; self-sows to maintain a permanent colony with minimal management in appropriate conditions
description.deciduousEvergreen: Annual — frost-killed; self-sows for the next year
description.winterInterest: None; frost-killed; may self-sow seeds visible on the soil surface

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun preferred; tolerates part shade (3-4 hours sun) while still blooming, though less prolifically; somewhat unusual for an Asteraceae in its shade tolerance
lightAndWater.water: Low to medium; drought tolerant once established; actually blooms better in slightly lean, dry conditions than in rich, moist soil

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained to average; tolerates lean conditions; performs in a range of soil types

claySoilPerformance.description: Tolerates moderately heavy clay if not waterlogged; in compacted clay, blooms may be reduced and powdery mildew risk increases — raised beds or amended soil improves performance significantly

soil.texturePreference: Adaptable; average to well-drained soil; lean soil often produces more blooms than rich amended soil; performs in sandy loam, loam, and moderately heavy soils
soil.rootCharacter: Fibrous annual root system; taprooted; does not transplant well after germination — direct sow in place

bloom.bloomTime: Spring through fall frost — one of the longest-blooming annuals available; in Zone 6 typically blooms May through November with regular deadheading; in Zone 8-9 may bloom nearly year-round with a pause in peak midsummer heat; in Zone 4-5 blooms from June until first hard frost
bloom.bloomDescription: Daisy-like flowers 2-3" wide in shades of orange, yellow, gold, and apricot; the typical calendar marigold orange is the most common; single and double forms available; the flowers close at night and on cloudy days — a charming characteristic
bloom.fragrance: Foliage strongly resinous-aromatic; flowers mildly fragrant; the whole plant has a distinctive sticky, resinous scent
bloom.pollination: Excellent pollinator plant — bees, hoverflies, butterflies, and beneficial insects work calendula intensively throughout its very long bloom season; one of the best annual pollinator plants per unit time (season-long bloom)

yield.edibleParts: Petals (edible — used as a saffron substitute for color in soups, rice, and baked goods; mild, slightly bitter flavor; the orange color is striking and useful as a natural food colorant); young leaves (edible in salads — bitter)
yield.harvestSeason: Flowers throughout the growing season; harvest fully open flowers in the morning; dry on screens immediately
yield.medicinalUses: One of the most important topical medicinal herbs — calendula oil and salve are the first treatments for skin irritation, minor wounds, burns, eczema, chapped skin, and diaper rash; well-documented anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties (flavonoids, triterpenoids, polysaccharides); calendula tea used for digestive inflammation and menstrual cramps; one of the top 10 most practical medicinal herbs for a home apothecary; commercially available in many pharmaceutical and cosmetic products
yield.craftOtherYield: Dried flowers for arrangements (retain color); infused oil (foundational preparation for medicinal salves and cosmetics); natural fabric dye (golden-yellow); dried petals as saffron substitute

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic; edible and widely used medicinally; caution for Asteraceae allergies; patch-test before extensive topical use
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Generally safe; skin contact may cause mild irritation in sensitive animals
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: Calendula root exudates are documented to deter nematodes and some soilborne pathogens (particularly Fusarium and nematodes); this is a positive companion planting effect — a natural biofumigant in the root zone

roles: Food (petals as culinary colorant and garnish)
Medicinal (topical wound healing — one of the most practically useful medicinal herbs in the home apothecary)
Pest Confuser (resinous aromatic foliage and root exudates deter pests and nematodes)
Ground Cover (dense planting fills ground spaces)
companionPlants: Tomatoes (classic companion — root exudates deter tomato root nematodes)

vegetables and herbs throughout the food garden; excellent throughout guild edges and annual ground layers; pairs well with any plant that benefits from nematode suppression and season-long insectary support
antagonists: Fennel (controversial — some sources suggest mild incompatibility); do not overcrowd in high-humidity conditions (prone to powdery mildew when airflow is poor)
permaculture.coppiceable: Deadhead consistently — remove spent flowers before seed set to maintain continuous bloom; if left to set seed, the plant focuses on seed production and bloom production decreases; allow a few plants to self-seed for next year's colony

wildlife.attracts: Bees (excellent, season-long), hoverflies (important predator of aphids — their larvae are voracious aphid consumers), beneficial wasps, butterflies; the long bloom season makes calendula one of the most consistently valuable pollinator plants across the entire gardening season
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Not a significant host plant for native Lepidoptera
wildlife.nestingHabitat: None significant; the dense mats of self-seeded plants provide some ground cover for invertebrates

deer.description: Good — the strong resinous fragrance generally deters deer browsing; not a primary browse target across most of the lower 48; may be sampled under high deer pressure when preferred forage is scarce

drought.description: Good — tolerates lean, dry conditions; actually blooms better in slightly lean soil; suitable for low-water gardens across the lower 48

tolerances.other: Tolerates light frost (cold-hardy annual); actually prefers cool weather (blooms best in spring and fall rather than midsummer heat — may pause or decline in Zones 7-9 during peak summer heat before rebounding in fall); self-sows reliably to establish a permanent self-renewing colony across a wide range of climates

maintenance.pruningNotes: Deadhead consistently for maximum bloom — remove spent flower heads before they set seed; a few minutes of deadheading per week maintains flowering through the season; allow the final flush of fall flowers to set seed for next year's colony; the seeds germinate readily in spring and the colony self-perpetuates; in lean soil, no fertilization needed
propagationMethods: Seed (direct sow in place in early spring, as soon as soil can be worked; tolerates frost; germinates in cool soil; does not transplant well after germination; self-sows reliably from established plantings; in Zones 8-9, fall sowing for winter/spring bloom is also effective)

cultivarNotes: Named cultivars vary primarily in flower size and doubling. **'Pacific Beauty'** (mixed colors — orange, yellow, apricot; widely available, reliable, good for drying; suitable across the lower 48 as a workhorse annual). **'Indian Prince'** (deep orange with purple undersides — striking; best single-color selection for bold orange-themed plantings). **'Flashback'** (pastel mixed colors — pale apricot, cream, pink-tinged; lower contrast, softer look). **'Touch of Red'** (red-tipped petals — interesting accent). **'Radio'** (quilled petals — unusual form, good for cut flowers). For hot-summer climates (Zones 7-9), look for heat-tolerant strains; standard cultivars may go dormant mid-summer in the South and Gulf Coast but rebound in fall. In cold climates (Zones 4-5), direct sow as early as possible in spring — the cool germination requirement is an advantage. Across the lower 48, 'Pacific Beauty' is the safest all-regions selection; 'Indian Prince' performs reliably wherever calendula grows well.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Versatile annual suitable as guild ground layer, kitchen garden edging, or pollinator border filler across all lower 48 climates. Performs best at guild and bed edges where self-seeding can be managed; the continuous season-long bloom contributes insectary value anywhere it is placed; medicinal harvest is practical from any accessible planting. In food forest systems, use as the annual ground cover layer at guild edges. In ornamental settings, pair with bold perennials as a long-blooming filler.

missouriBotanicalGarden.url:
https://www.missouribotanicalgarden.org/PlantFinder/PlantFinderDetails.aspx?kempercode=a566
Pennsylvania — Hegins Site Observations:
- Annual self-seeding colony in red clay soil possible at bed edges and disturbed areas, though germination is more reliable in amended or raised bed soil
- Deer pressure in Hegins is active; resinous foliage provides meaningful deterrence — calendula is low-priority browse compared to many other annuals
- Humidity in the humid continental climate increases powdery mildew risk in dense plantings; maintain airflow by not overcrowding; lean soil reduces lush growth that is most susceptible
- Blooms best in the cool springs and falls of Zone 6a; may slow in peak July-August heat but typically does not stop entirely; the long PA growing season is well-matched to calendula's bloom habit
- Self-sowing colony management: allow the last flush of fall flowers to set seed, then leave seed heads in place; seeds overwinter in the soil and germinate in early spring with no intervention needed

pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not listed as invasive in Pennsylvania
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: In Hegins red clay, direct sowing into raised beds or amended soil edges is preferred; in compacted native clay, germination and vigor are reduced; self-seeding is more reliable in looser disturbed soil at bed edges
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Red clay in Hegins benefits from at least a surface layer of compost or wood chip mulch to allow self-seeded germination; direct sow works reliably at bed edges and disturbed soil areas where clay has been loosened
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Standard cultivars perform excellently in Hegins conditions. Direct sow in early April (soil workable but still cool) for first bloom by late May-June; allow fall-setting seeds for a self-sustaining colony. 'Pacific Beauty' and 'Indian Prince' are both well-suited to the Zone 6a season length and the cool spring/fall periods when calendula blooms most prolifically.
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Mulberry guild annual ground layer and kitchen herb strip (Section B). Self-seeding calendula colony in the mulberry guild provides season-long insectary value (bees, hoverflies, beneficial wasps); medicinal harvest is highly practical from the guild area; the orange/yellow flowers complement the mulberry fruit color palette; root exudates contribute to soil health in the guild. Also suitable along south-facing bed edges (Section C) for visual color and medicinal access.
